extraction_policy.node_density: Extract ALL significant entities from the text — do not limit yourself to only the most prominent ones. Minor concepts, supporting methods, and secondary tools should also be extracted if mentioned.
extraction_policy.no_self_loops: Do NOT create an edge where start and target are the same node.
extraction_policy.confidence_note: Use confidence 0.3-0.5 for weak/indirect relations, 0.7-1.0 for strong/explicit ones. Do NOT omit edges just because confidence is low.

node_fields.name: string (required, unique)
node_fields.type: string | array<string> (required, one or more of node_types)
node_fields.keywords: string[] (optional). Up to 2 concise keywords that aid search or disambiguation. Omit if not useful.

edge_fields.description: string (optional). How or why the relationship exists.
edge_fields.evidence: string (optional). Verbatim quote from source validating this relationship.
edge_fields.confidence: number (0.0-1.0, optional). Use 0.3-0.5 for weak/indirect, 0.7-1.0 for strong/explicit.

node_types.name: Concept
node_types.description: An abstract idea, principle, ideology, or category that cannot be directly executed or applied as a procedure. Use when the node represents a 'what' or 'why', not a 'how'. e.g., 'Entropy', 'Nationalism'. NOT: named procedures or algorithms (->Method), named theoretical frameworks (->Theory).

node_types.name: Theory
node_types.description: A named theoretical framework or school of thought that explains or predicts phenomena. e.g., 'Darwin's Theory of Evolution', 'Keynesian Economics', 'Structuralism'. NOT: formally proven mathematical/logical statements (->Theorem), specific quantitative representations (->Model), abstract ideas without a formal name (->Concept).

node_types.name: Method
node_types.description: A named analytical technique, experimental approach, or non-computational procedure. e.g., 'Regression Analysis', 'Case Study Method', 'Discourse Analysis', 'Double-Blind Trial'. NOT: computational algorithms with well-defined steps (->Algorithm), abstract concepts (->Concept).

node_types.name: Algorithm
node_types.description: A named computational or mathematical step-by-step procedure with well-defined inputs and outputs. e.g., 'Dijkstra's Algorithm', 'Quicksort', 'Backpropagation', 'Gaussian Elimination'. NOT: broad analytical techniques (->Method), abstract concepts (->Concept).

node_types.name: Theorem
node_types.description: A formally proven or provable statement in mathematics, CS, or formal sciences. e.g., 'CAP Theorem', 'Pythagorean Theorem', 'Coase Theorem', 'Pumping Lemma'. NOT: broad theoretical frameworks (->Theory), abstract ideas (->Concept).

node_types.name: Model
node_types.description: A simplified formal representation of a system or phenomenon used for prediction or simulation. e.g., 'IS-LM Model', 'Solow Growth Model', 'BERT', 'Standard Model of Physics'. NOT: broad theoretical frameworks or schools of thought (->Theory), formally proven statements (->Theorem), step-by-step procedures (->Algorithm/Method).

node_types.name: Formula
node_types.description: A mathematical equation, formal symbolic expression, or quantitative rule. e.g., 'E = mc²', 'Bayes' Rule', 'Black-Scholes Equation'

node_types.name: Problem
node_types.description: A challenge, limitation, conflict, bottleneck, or open question in any domain. e.g., 'Overfitting', 'Income Inequality', 'Climate Change'

node_types.name: Person
node_types.description: A researcher, scientist, historical figure, politician, or author associated with a concept, discovery, or event. e.g., 'Alan Turing', 'Karl Marx', 'Marie Curie'

node_types.name: Organization
node_types.description: A named institution, company, government body, political party, state, empire, or international group. e.g., 'United Nations', 'Chinese Communist Party'. NOT: software libraries or frameworks (->Tool).

node_types.name: Event
node_types.description: A specific historical, political, social, or scientific occurrence at a defined point or period. e.g., 'French Revolution', 'World War II', 'Moon Landing', '2008 Financial Crisis'

node_types.name: Place
node_types.description: A geographic location, region, or territory. e.g., 'Paris', 'Korean Peninsula', 'Silicon Valley'. NOT: political entities acting as agents (->Organization).

node_types.name: Period
node_types.description: A historical era, timeframe, or defined phase. e.g., 'Cold War', 'Industrial Revolution', 'Joseon Dynasty', 'Renaissance'

node_types.name: Document
node_types.description: A named law, treaty, declaration, policy, academic paper, or formal text with concrete impact. e.g., 'Treaty of Versailles', 'Magna Carta', 'GDPR'. NOT: project titles, course assignments, or topic names (->Concept).

node_types.name: Tool
node_types.description: A specific software, library, framework, instrument, dataset, or named resource. e.g., 'TensorFlow', 'scikit-learn'. NOT: organizations or institutions (->Organization).

node_types.name: Example
node_types.description: A concrete, worked instance explicitly used to illustrate a Concept, Theory, or Method. Use only when the source presents it as an example or case study.

edge_types.name: defines
edge_types.description: start provides a formal definition of or introduces target as a concept. Direction: DEFINER → defines → DEFINED. NOT: founding or establishing an organization/movement (->leads_to).

edge_types.name: uses
edge_types.description: start depends on or applies target in its operation or practice. Direction: USER → uses → USED. Do NOT use when start is applied within a domain (->applied_in).
edge_types.example: Regression Analysis → uses → Least Squares Method

edge_types.name: part_of
edge_types.description: start is a sub-element or component of target. Direction: SMALLER → part_of → LARGER. Never reverse.

edge_types.name: causes
edge_types.description: start directly triggers target. Direction: CAUSE → causes → EFFECT. Never reverse. Use only for direct, immediate causation.

edge_types.name: influences
edge_types.description: start indirectly shapes target without being a direct cause. Direction: INFLUENCER → influences → INFLUENCED. Use when the effect is gradual or indirect.

edge_types.name: leads_to
edge_types.description: start progresses toward target sequentially. Use for process flows or step-by-step pipelines where one step produces the next. Direction: EARLIER → leads_to → LATER. Do NOT use for indirect influence (->influences).

edge_types.name: solves
edge_types.description: start fully addresses or resolves target (a Problem). Use 'improves' if only partial. Direction: SOLUTION → solves → PROBLEM.

edge_types.name: improves
edge_types.description: start reduces a limitation of target without fully solving it. Direction: IMPROVEMENT → improves → TARGET.
edge_types.example: Deep Learning → improves → Traditional Machine Learning

edge_types.name: requires
edge_types.description: start cannot function without target as a hard prerequisite. Direction: DEPENDENT → requires → DEPENDENCY. Never reverse.

edge_types.name: prerequisite_of
edge_types.description: Understanding start is pedagogically necessary before learning target. Direction: EARLIER KNOWLEDGE → prerequisite_of → LATER KNOWLEDGE.

edge_types.name: derives_from
edge_types.description: start is mathematically, logically, or historically derived from target. Direction: DERIVED → derives_from → SOURCE.

edge_types.name: equivalent_to
edge_types.description: start and target express the same idea in different forms. Bidirectional — add only one direction.
edge_types.example: Natural Selection → equivalent_to → Survival of the Fittest

edge_types.name: example_of
edge_types.description: start is a concrete instantiation of target. Direction: INSTANCE → example_of → GENERAL.

edge_types.name: contrasts_with
edge_types.description: start and target differ in approach, assumptions, or trade-offs. Bidirectional — add only one direction.

edge_types.name: applied_in
edge_types.description: start (a Concept, Theory, or Method) is practically used within a domain, field, or real-world context (not another Method or Tool). Direction: CONCEPT/THEORY/METHOD → applied_in → DOMAIN/FIELD/CONTEXT. Do NOT use when target is another Method or Tool (->uses).

edge_types.name: occurs_in
edge_types.description: Event takes place at a specific Place. Direction: EVENT → occurs_in → PLACE.

edge_types.name: happens_during
edge_types.description: Event or sub-event occurs within the timeframe of a broader Period or larger Event. Direction: SUB-EVENT → happens_during → BROADER PERIOD/EVENT.
edge_types.example: D-Day Invasion → happens_during → World War II

edge_types.name: proposed_by
edge_types.description: start (Theory/Model/Theorem/Algorithm) was developed or proposed by target (Person). Direction: THEORY/MODEL/THEOREM → proposed_by → PERSON.
